metrics.narrative: The buyer articulated operational pain around manual contract tracking in their legacy ERP system (Business Central / EnergyOne) but has not quantified the financial impact (cost, cycle time, headcount, error rate, or savings). On the 10 Feb 2026 discovery call the buyer described the current manual tracking burden and the need for automated obligation tracking but stopped short of stating ROI or hard metrics. The 13 Apr demo call surfaced the specific product gap (obligation tracking) that Zycus must build within 12 months, but no financial value case was presented by either side. The absence of quantified metrics means the deal lacks a defensible ROI story to secure executive sign-off, and without an Economic Buyer engaged, no one has validated the business case. Quantifying the value case and presenting it to the budget holder is the critical next move.

champion.narrative: Luke Davies was identified as the champion in the early discovery phase but has gone passive. He attended the 10 Feb 2026 discovery call and was described in the Next Step as a champion, but he has not appeared on any subsequent call (29 Apr demo, 13 May feedback) and has not been mentioned in any task or next-step update since February. Chitra Shende (Contract Manager) is the most actively engaged contact, attending all three calls (10 Feb, 29 Apr, 13 May) and driving the RFP demo and feedback cycle, but her role is operational, not strategic: she has no documented access to the Economic Buyer or Decision Maker (Nick Middleton), no evidence of internal advocacy (no introductions, no pushing back on competitors, no driving the timeline), and no executive sponsorship. The 24 Jun task notes the buyer will reengage 'shortly' but Chitra did not proactively reach out; the deal is reactive, not champion-driven. Ahmad Salar (contact role, attended 10 Feb call) has no documented role beyond initial discovery. The champion situation is weak and cooling: the original champion is disengaged, the active contact is an operational executor with no strategic influence, and no one is advocating for Zycus internally. Rebuilding the champion relationship and securing executive access is critical.

competition.narrative: Zycus is competing against Ironclad and the status quo (Business Central / EnergyOne manual tracking). Ironclad was named on the 10 Feb 2026 discovery call as the primary competitor in the shortlisted vendor evaluation. The critical competitive wedge is obligation tracking: on the 13 Apr 2026 demo call the buyer surfaced that Zycus CLM does not currently support obligation tracking out of the box, and requested a time-bound roadmap commitment for a 12-month feature build. This is a direct product gap. If Ironclad offers stronger out-of-the-box obligation tracking capability (the buyer's stated primary requirement), Ironclad is ahead on the buyer's decision criteria. Zycus's counter-wedge must be (a) a credible, milestone-driven 12-month roadmap commitment for obligation tracking, (b) superior ERP integration capability (the buyer raised integration questions on 11 May and 24 Jun, signaling this is a decision factor), and (c) broader CLM platform strength (AI/Merlin capabilities, if the buyer has appetite). However, the AI positioning is undocumented in the calls, and the roadmap commitment has been requested but not confirmed delivered or accepted by the buyer. The competitive position is exposed: Zycus is behind on the primary use case and has not closed the gap with a winning wedge. How we win: deliver the obligation-tracking roadmap commitment with named milestones and Executive Sponsor sign-off, demonstrate superior ERP integration in the promised technical session, and position Merlin AI capabilities as Phase 2 value if the buyer has AI appetite (unknown).
